TION, AND IMPLEMENTATION TOOLS IN ELECTRONIC COURSEWARE FOR TEACHING SIGNAL PROCESSING Roberto H. Bamberger, Brian L. Evans, Edward A. Lee, James H. McClellan and Mark A. Yoder Proceedings IEEE Int. Conf. on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing,

onf. on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing, pp. 2873-2876 Detroit, MI, May 8-12, 1995 Prepublished version Published version ABSTRACT A typical path in learning digital signal processing begins at the theoretical end and progresses toward the p

at the theoretical end and progresses toward the practical constraints imposed by implementation in hardware or software. On this path, the student would learn how to convert mathematical theory into algorithms and then algorithms into efficient impl

algorithms and then algorithms into efficient implementations. In this paper, we first summarize the electronic courseware we have already developed in Mathematica, Matlab , and Ptolemy to teach DSP theory, algorithms, and implementation, respectivel
heory, algorithms, and implementation, respectively. Then, we discuss ways to integrate our efforts to help students discover the connections between these topics.
